diagnosis_result.doctor_diagnosis: This 59-year-old male with a history of alcohol-induced cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma was admitted for a deceased donor liver transplant. His postoperative course was complex, requiring a surgical re-exploration for suspected vascular obstruction, management of new-onset atrial fibrillation and flutter with digoxin, and a pericardial window for a large pericardial effusion. Additionally, he underwent an ERCP with biliary stenting for a bile leak and CT-guided placement of an abdominal pigtail catheter to manage a bilious collection. Despite experiencing transient postoperative delirium that resolved before discharge, the patient's condition stabilized, and he was alert, coherent, and independently ambulatory at the time of transition. He was discharged home with an immunosuppressive regimen of tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and a prednisone taper, alongside prophylactic medications and digoxin for rate control. Home health services were arranged to assist with his abdominal drain management, and he will continue close follow-up with the transplant clinic for frequent lab monitoring and further care.
diagnosis_result.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 59-year-old male who was admitted for a liver transplant due to HCC/ETOH cirrhosis. He has a past medical history of EtOH cirrhosis, skin cancer, and has undergone TIPS and RFA procedures. He has no known allergies or adverse reactions. The patient underwent a deceased donor liver transplant and was intubated post-operatively due to respiratory failure. He was later extubated and transferred to the floor for post-operative care. During his hospital stay, he developed atrial fibrillation, which was treated with digoxin and propranolol. He also developed a pericardial effusion, which was drained and treated with antibiotics. The patient's laboratory results showed normal liver function tests, and he was prescribed immunosuppressive medications, including Cellcept, myfortic, and tacrolimus. He was discharged with a drain in the mid abdominal collection and was advised to follow up with his healthcare provider for further treatment.
